data.summary: Cross-project comms: global-default root + auto-filled project field; v0.3.0
data.detail: v0.3.0 makes teammate-comms global by default so agents in different projects can message each other. resolve_comms_root order changed to comms_dir > $TEAMMATE_COMMS_DIR > $CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR > ~/.claude; $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is no longer the default root (it isolated agents per repo) and is instead used to auto-fill a new optional `project` profile field (basename of the project dir, overridable). Per-project isolation stays available via $TEAMMATE_COMMS_DIR. The `project` field shows always in teammate_list (with status/authority), plus teammate_profile and whoami. Tests + docs updated; flags the flat-namespace name-collision tradeoff (agent inbox names must be unique across all projects). 5 profile fields now: project/role/personality/status/authority.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: teammate-comms comms root is global by default (~/.claude), not per-project
data.detail: Chosen so agents across different projects can message each other out of the box. resolve_comms_root order: comms_dir > $TEAMMATE_COMMS_DIR > $CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR > ~/.claude. Rejected: keeping $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR as default (isolated agents per repo). Per-project isolation still available via $TEAMMATE_COMMS_DIR.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: New `project` profile field auto-filled from basename($CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR), overridable
data.detail: With a global comms root, teammates need to see who is in which repo. `project` is auto-filled at registration from the project dir (since a plugin-spawned server's cwd is the cache dir, $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is the reliable source). Always shown in teammate_list alongside status/authority. Chosen auto-fill over manual so agents don't forget to set it.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Global comms namespace = agent inbox names must be unique across ALL projects
data.detail: Because the default comms root is now global (~/.claude), two agents in different repos both named e.g. 'lead' collide on one inbox and fight over it (the server's same-name collision warning applies). `project` is metadata only; it does not namespace inboxes. Team namespacing carves out subsets if needed.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: vibe-cognition /plugin install fails EPERM-rename when a running server locks its plugin cache dir (Windows)
data.detail: `/plugin install vibe-cognition@coltondyck` failed repeatedly with `EPERM: operation not permitted, rename ...temp_git_* -> ...cache/coltondyck/vibe-cognition/0.5.1`. Root cause: a live vibe_cognition.server (uv.exe→.venv python) spawned by a project .mcp.json held file handles in the populated target cache dir; plugin wasn't in installed_plugins.json so install did a fresh clone→rename onto the locked dir → Windows denies it. 5 stray temp_git_* leftovers from repeated attempts.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Unblock recipe for the plugin-cache install lock: kill server PIDs, delete stale cache + temp_git_*, reinstall
data.detail: Verified fix: (1) find + Stop-Process the python/uv PIDs running vibe_cognition.server from the cache dir; (2) delete the stale cache/coltondyck/vibe-cognition/<ver> dir and any temp_git_* leftovers; (3) re-run /plugin install. Safe because the cognition GRAPH lives under the project's REPO_PATH/.cognition, NOT in the plugin cache (which is just code + .venv, recreated on reinstall).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: vibe-cognition wiring smell: no mcpServers in plugin.json; per-project .mcp.json points into the versioned plugin cache dir
data.detail: vibe-cognition's plugin.json has no mcpServers block, so the plugin never auto-spawns its server; each project wires it via a manual .mcp.json that runs the server with --directory into the cache dir and an explicit REPO_PATH (server reads only REPO_PATH, falling back to cwd; no $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR awareness). This couples the manual server to the cache path the plugin system owns and replaces — the cause of the install-lock incident.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Deferred durable fix for vibe-cognition wiring: mcpServers via ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} + default REPO_PATH to $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR
data.detail: Plan (deferred per user until agents are back up so no graph history is lost): give vibe-cognition's plugin.json its own mcpServers block using ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}, default REPO_PATH to $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR when unset, and delete the per-project .mcp.json files. Mirrors how teammate-comms is wired. Eliminates the cache-path coupling that causes the install lock. Vince (vibe-cognition code owner) will implement when greenlit.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Shipped server is pure-stdlib zero-dep JSON-RPC, NOT the mcp-SDK path DESIGN §6 led with — took the documented stdlib fallback
data.detail: DESIGN.md §6 framed an mcp-SDK low-level Server (deps mcp>=1.27) as primary, owning the ServerSession to push unsolicited channel events, with pure-stdlib as a 'documented fallback'. The implementation took the fallback: server.py is newline-delimited JSON-RPC over stdio, hand-rolled tools/list+tools/call, raw stdout under a lock, background daemon thread for the channel. pyproject dependencies=[] (zero third-party runtime deps) — keeps the MCP spawn instant.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: DESIGN.md is the original build blueprint and is now partially STALE vs shipped reality — trust code over DESIGN.md
data.detail: DESIGN.md still says 'No plugin code exists yet' and describes choices that didn't ship: mcp>=1.27 dependency (actual: zero-dep stdlib), a cli.py (never built), a plugin.json env block (removed), identity via $TEAMMATE_AGENT (actual: teammate_register tool), and §4b's old coltondyck-collision handling (superseded by the colton-claude-plugins consolidation). §8/§9 were updated for v0.3.0 (global root, profile fields) but the rest is historical. Use it for rationale/lineage, not current API truth.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Identity established at runtime via teammate_register tool, not baked into launch config; $TEAMMATE_AGENT is only a convenience auto-register
data.detail: Server starts identity-less; teammate_register(agent,...) resolves the comms root, registers the inbox/record, and arms the channel watcher. Other messaging tools return isError until registered. If $TEAMMATE_AGENT is set the server auto-registers at startup as a shortcut. Shift from DESIGN §10's env-baked identity — chosen because unset ${VAR} in plugin mcpServers.env made Claude Code reject the config.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Channel wake = background watcher polls own _unread.json every 0.5s, baseline-seeded at init, emits notifications/claude/channel on count rise
data.detail: channel.py runs a daemon thread gated on notifications/initialized AND registration. It seeds baseline to the current unread count at init (so pre-existing messages don't nudge — the agent drains them once on startup), emits a channel event only when count rises above baseline, resets baseline down on ack, and heartbeats the registry every 5s. The inbox JSON is the source of truth: a dropped/missed push loses nothing, recovered on next teammate_inbox.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Concurrency-safety trio in comms.py: atomic write (os.replace), cross-platform mkdir lock, non-destructive read
data.detail: write_json_atomic writes a .tmp then os.replace (atomic on same volume, incl. Windows). file_lock acquires via atomic mkdir of a .lock dir (works on all OSes) with a 10s timeout + forced eviction; file_lock_optional is a best-effort variant that returns False instead of raising (used by registry writes so a heartbeat collision drops the beat rather than erroring). read_json_readonly never rewrites on a partial/corrupt read, so a poller can't destroy a message mid-delivery.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: stdio transport must be BOM-free UTF-8 terminated by \n; input read tolerant of a leading BOM (Windows)
data.detail: The JSON-RPC stdio stream writes each message as one BOM-free UTF-8 line + \n under a stdout lock (no CRLF, no cp1252), and decodes stdin with utf-8-sig to tolerate a leading BOM. No handler may print to stdout (that's the protocol stream); diagnostics go to stderr (~/.claude/debug/<session>.txt).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Channel liveness: same-host authoritative pid check, else heartbeat freshness (30s window); teammate_list uses heartbeat-only to avoid N tasklist subprocesses
data.detail: is_channel_alive: if record.host == this host and pid_check=True, use OS-level pid liveness (tasklist on Windows, os.kill(0) elsewhere) — authoritative, no staleness window. Otherwise (cross-host, undetermined, or pid_check=False) fall back to heartbeat freshness within 30s. teammate_list passes pid_check=False so listing N agents doesn't spawn N tasklist subprocesses. Heartbeat is written every 5s by the channel watcher.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: PLANNED (not built): teammate_reincarnate — MCP tool spawns a new full Claude instance in a project dir as a (possibly offline) named teammate

data.summary: Shipped group chat (v0.4.0): named #-sigil groups, fan-out wake + shared transcript, teammate_group tool
data.detail: Implemented + shipped the group-chat feature planned earlier (supersedes the PLANNED node). Release commit 39f3fc9, v0.4.0; both marketplaces re-pinned (in-repo colton-comms @ 2812109, consolidated colton-claude-plugins @ 418b1f1) and verified live. Design as built: groups addressed with a # sigil (teammate_send(to="#design")) — separate namespace from agents, branch in _handle_send is on syntax before validate_agent_name (which rejects #). comms.py gained get_groups_dir/get_group_dir, validate_group_name, read/write_group_meta, append_group_message, read_group_messages, delete_group (per-group subdir groups/<g>/{meta.json,messages.json}; all reads via read_json_readonly). New teammate_group tool with action enum create/delete/join/leave/add/members/history (action validated in handler — clients don't enforce enum). Fan-out: transcript written first (authoritative), then best-effort per-member inbox append (locked inbox non-fatal), skip sender, auto-join sender (open membership), liveness pid_check=False. _handle_inbox tags [group: #X]; _handle_list has a Groups section. Bug found+fixed during test: file_lock needs the parent dir to exist (mkdir parents=False on the .lock) — create must mkdir the group dir before locking meta.json. Peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent before coding (caught namespace collision, read_json_safe footgun, tasklist blowup, open-vs-required-member contradiction). test_handshake.py extended to 9 tools + group create/dup/fan-out/transcript/history/bad-action/inbox-tag; all green.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Group-chat v0.4.0 live-test feedback: top fixes = kill sender self-wake + make history canonical; wishlist typed posts/@mentions/threading
data.detail: Live multi-agent test of group chat (Silvie + Lord-Wellington + Nancy). Worked end-to-end (fan-out, channel wake, transcript, leave/rejoin). Feedback for a future polish pass: (1) SELF-WAKE NOISE — agents report being channel-nudged right after their own post then opening an empty inbox; I also saw 'nudge then empty inbox' (in my case caused by ack-all clearing messages that streamed in between read and ack). Investigate: fan-out skips the sender, so a true self-wake shouldn't occur — likely a channel baseline false-nudge worth fixing. (2) HISTORY AS CANONICAL — inbox copy is ephemeral (ack 'all' removes group messages); teammate_group history is the only reliable record. Make history first-class/greppable (filter by group + sender). Lord-Wellington: 'inbox is the doorbell, history is the file.' (3) UX: bold/glyph the [group: #X] tag; inbox header hint 'reply with to:#group'. (4) Wishlist for scale: typed posts (decision/blocker/FYI/chatter) so history is a decision trail, @mentions to pull one owner into a thread, threading, mute/snooze, read receipts. ack-all-mid-stream is itself a footgun (acks unread arrivals).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Shipped group-chat fixes v0.4.1: ack-all keeps unseen arrivals; history sender filter; tag glyph
data.detail: Acted on live group-chat test feedback (cognition d10dbc1f3683). Release d8e3131, v0.4.1; both marketplaces re-pinned (in-repo 2d41e58, consolidated colton-claude-plugins 8a42361) and verified live. Peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent before coding — the review corrected my root-cause framing. KEY FINDING: the testers' 'self-wake' is NOT a real self-wake (channel watcher polls only the agent's own _unread.json; group fan-out skips the sender). The real bug was teammate_ack('all') sweeping up messages that arrived after the last read (read-without-reading), which also produced the 'nudge then empty inbox' symptom. Fix: ack('all') now clears only messages SEEN as of the last full teammate_inbox read; later arrivals are preserved. Implemented via a session-local last-seen id SET on Identity (server.py) with dedicated lock-guarded set_last_seen/get_last_seen — snapshot()'s 4-tuple left untouched (5 unpack sites), last-seen sentinel None=never-read (ack-all then clears all, preserving startup-drain), cleared to None on identity change; teammate_inbox records ids AFTER the count_only early return. Also: teammate_group history gained a sender filter (filter-then-limit) + advertised as the canonical record in the tool description and channel nudge; inbox group tag now [👥 group: #X] (kept the 'group:' substring so the test held). IMPORTANT scope call (peer review): fully eliminating stale/empty nudges needs channel.py coalescing, which risks MISSED nudges if naive — deferred to the v0.5 polish plan. Tests: ack-all-preserves-post-read-arrival, history sender filter, glyph present; 9 tools, all green.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: PLANNED (not built): v0.5 group-chat polish — typed posts, @mentions, history-as-decision-trail, nudge coalescing, mute, read receipts
data.detail: Peer-reviewed plan at ~/.claude/plans/teammate-group-chat-v05-polish.md. Order by value/risk: (1) typed posts decision/blocker/FYI/chatter (type field + history type filter; turns transcript into a decision trail; no new tool); (3) history since-cursor + combined filters; THEN the nudge-layer cluster — (2) @mentions need PER-MEMBER fan-out records + a priority-aware channel (today the watcher only counts), (4) stale-nudge coalescing must tie re-nudge to read position (reuse v0.4.1 last-seen) to avoid missed-nudge regressions, (5) mute via group meta; last (6) read receipts (write-on-read inverts the read-cheap design — heavy), (7) threading. Principle: extend teammate_send/teammate_group args, no new tools; additive record fields; test for MISSED nudges not just extra ones.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: v0.4.1 cross-validated by 2 live testers: selective ack-all PROVEN (live race "Kept N"); 👥 glyph + history sender filter confirmed; self-wake/count-padding noise still live → v0.5
data.detail: Second live group-chat test round on v0.4.1 (Silvie + Lord-Wellington + Nancy, all on v0.4.1). Independently validated by both peers: (1) ack("all") selective clear PROVEN end-to-end in live traffic — Nancy accidentally staged the full race (read 2, 3rd landed mid-stream, ack-all → "Acknowledged 2 seen. Kept 1 that arrived since your last read", 3rd intact on re-read); Wellington caught it live twice ("Kept 1", "Kept 2"). (2) 👥 group tag glyph confirmed it pops on skim. (3) teammate_group history sender filter confirmed zero-bleed, both call it the canonical decision-trail win. NEW REPRO for the deferred nudge issue: BOTH testers report being woken to count=2 with FEWER real unread right after their OWN outgoing posts, plus per-increment re-nudge as the counter climbs 1→2→3. This nuances the earlier "self-wake cannot happen" conclusion: the sender is still skipped in fan-out (no self-write), so the likely cause is the channel emitting on EVERY count rise + a stale count display at wake time, not a literal self-write — but two matching reports warrant real verification in the v0.5 coalescing work, not assumption. Process note: an EP on reflex inbox-drain read a "don't read this" probe before honoring it — selective-ack is robust to that (held the in-flight msgs anyway).
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Shipped v0.4.2: channel nudges gated on read-position — kills the self-wake/count-padding noise
data.detail: Fixed the noise both v0.4.1 testers confirmed (cognition d771ab975ca3). Release b64ab89, v0.4.2; both marketplaces re-pinned (in-repo 8201cf6, consolidated colton-claude-plugins b82401e) + verified live. Peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent, which confirmed the design is missed-nudge-safe. Root cause was NOT a self-wake (fan-out skips the sender) — channel.py used an integer baseline that re-nudged on every unread-count rise and counted read-but-unacked messages, so you'd be woken to count=2 with one genuinely-new message. Fix: run_watcher now gates nudges on read-position, reusing v0.4.1's Identity.last_seen — nudge only for unread ids that are neither already-seen (last_seen, set by teammate_inbox) nor already-nudged (a watcher-local known_ids set seeded to the inbox at registration so pre-existing messages don't nudge); emitted count = len(unread - last_seen) so a read-but-unacked message never pads it; reading (not acking) silences a nudge. Missed-nudge-safe because a genuinely new message has a fresh forward-unique id in neither set. Updated channel.py docstring + DESIGN.md §7; test asserts nudge counts are never padded (all '1' in the harness flow). This supersedes the read-position part of the deferred v0.5 'nudge coalescing' item; true batch coalescing (one nudge for N simultaneous arrivals) remains an optional later refinement, no longer a user-facing pain.

data.summary: PLANNED v0.4.4: group nudge should name reply target for ANY unseen group message, not just the fresh trigger (mixed-batch edge)
data.detail: Edge case found live by Lord-Wellington during v0.4.3 verification, confirmed real against the code. v0.4.3 computes fresh_groups from `fresh = unread_ids - known_ids - last_seen` (the messages triggering THIS wake, excluding already-nudged). So: an unread group message that was already nudged earlier + a later DM → the DM-triggered wake correctly names no group, but the still-pending group thread isn't re-surfaced in that nudge (only the inbox [👥 group:] tag remains as a secondary signal). Decision (adopting Wellington's instinct; cost asymmetry favors never-fracture): broaden the groups computation from `fresh` to `unread_ids - last_seen` — name the group target if ANY unseen (unread, not-yet-read) message is group mail, regardless of what triggered the wake. Count stays unseen-only; the wake TRIGGER stays `fresh` (don't re-wake for already-nudged mail). One-line change in run_watcher (channel.py): fresh_groups → unseen_groups = {m.get('group') for m in messages if m.get('id') in (unread_ids - last_seen) and m.get('group')}. Ship as v0.4.4 (plan+peer-review per workflow). Note: this slightly revises the v0.4.3 peer-review guidance that said use `fresh` for groups — the mixed-batch fracture risk outweighs the "reflect only the trigger" tidiness.

data.summary: Built teammate_dashboard: stdlib web console + human-as-teammate + durable NDJSON transcript (0.5.0-dev, not released)
data.detail:
Implemented the peer-reviewed, user-approved plan for a 10th MCP tool, teammate_dashboard — a local Slack-style web console where the human becomes a first-class teammate. Pure-stdlib (http.server.ThreadingHTTPServer), zero new deps, mirroring vibe-cognition's dashboard PATTERN (token+Host auth, localhost bind, background daemon thread, single-file frontend, browser auto-open) without its Starlette/uvicorn stack.

Three additive halves, all backward-compatible:
1. comms.py: register_human (type:"human", inbox, NO pid/channel — never a wakeable channel, never trips the collision guard), set_human_presence (online/away), and an always-on append-only NDJSON observability log transcript.jsonl with get_transcript_file/append_transcript (best-effort, never-raise, file_lock_optional 2s, TEAMMATE_TRANSCRIPT=0 opt-out)/read_transcript (tail-limited).
2. tools.py: extracted SENDER-EXPLICIT cores send_dm/send_group from _handle_send/_send_to_group (now thin wrappers) so the dashboard posts AS the human, not the hosting process; tee transcript LAST (after delivery); send_group returns fan-out accounting; required type=="human" send footer; human marker (🧑 operator / presence=) in _handle_list/_format_profile; teammate_dashboard tool def + _handle_dashboard.
3. dashboard.py (new) + static/index.html (new): stdlib HTTP server (bind-first then register_human+online; idempotent per-process; shutdown→away+free port from server.py finally); REST /api/conversations, /api/poll?cursor= (short-poll 1.5s), /api/send; XSS-safe frontend (textContent only + strict CSP); token via window.__TOKEN + history.replaceState.

Peer-review fixes folded in pre-build: NDJSON not JSON-array (avoids O(n²)+whole-team lock), tee-LAST ordering, send_group accounting return, required human footer, dropped heartbeat timer (online@start/away@shutdown — deletes a thread-leak class), single poll endpoint, stdout-purity permanent test. Verified: test_handshake.py green (9→10 tools + new assertions for human record/marker, durable dm+group tee, stdout purity); a throwaway HTTP script confirmed conversations/poll/send round-trip, fan-out to a live agent's inbox, and every auth path (401 missing token, 403 bad Host, 403 index no-token, 200+CSP+token-substituted). Per the user's instruction: NO version bump, NO marketplace re-pin — local iteration only. Plan at ~/.claude/plans/something-i-want-to-cached-canyon.md.

data.summary: Reincarnate auto-detects the managed-settings channel allowlist; v0.6.5
data.detail: Shipped teammate-comms v0.6.5 (release commit 27bd405 on main; coltondyck marketplace re-pin 65ba2a8). teammate_reincarnate now auto-detects whether the operator has installed the managed-settings channel allowlist and launches spawned children the non-dangerous way when present. spawn.py changes: new managed_settings_paths() returns OS-specific managed-settings.json candidates (Windows %ProgramFiles%\ClaudeCode + %ProgramData%\ClaudeCode; macOS /Library/Application Support/ClaudeCode; Linux /etc/claude-code). New channel_allowlisted(plugin, marketplace, settings_paths=None) opens each path with utf-8-sig (BOM-tolerant), best-effort skips missing/unreadable/malformed (OSError covers PermissionError reading Program Files as non-admin; ValueError covers JSONDecodeError), and returns True iff channelsEnabled is truthy AND an allowedChannelPlugins entry matches plugin+marketplace. build_claude_command(prompt, extra_args, settings_paths) now selects ALLOWLISTED_LAUNCH_ARGS ("claude --channels plugin:teammate-comms@coltondyck", silent) when allowlisted, else DANGEROUS_LAUNCH_ARGS ("--dangerously-load-development-channels", which shows a trust prompt the DEVNULL-stdio child cannot answer). $TEAMMATE_LAUNCH_ARGS still overrides both verbatim. Design rationale: REQUIRE channelsEnabled so detection fails toward the dangerous flag — a wrong-False still loads the channel (maybe a prompt), whereas a wrong-True would launch with --channels and no dangerous flag and might not load the channel at all (worse). Constants refactored: PLUGIN_NAME/MARKETPLACE/PLUGIN_SPEC + DANGEROUS/ALLOWLISTED constants; DEFAULT_LAUNCH_ARGS kept as a back-compat alias of DANGEROUS. Tests (tests/test_handshake.py spawn block) made hermetic with injected settings_paths: dangerous-default (no allowlist), allowlist→--channels, channelsEnabled=false→not trusted, env-override-wins wrapped in try/finally so a failure can't leak TEAMMATE_LAUNCH_ARGS into later checks, plus a default-path channel_allowlisted() smoke-call so managed_settings_paths() ships tested; temp file via mkdtemp (not NamedTemporaryFile — Windows can't reopen an open handle). The existing hostile-prompt call was hardened to pass an absent settings path for determinism (Colton's machine now has a real managed-settings file). Docs: README "Trusting the channel (skip the dangerous flag)" section (per-OS path table + JSON + caveats: admin/elevation, machine-wide highest precedence, Notepad BOM); DESIGN.md + SKILL.md one-line pointers to it. Plan was peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent which flagged 3 ship-blockers (env try/finally, Windows temp-file handle, untested default-path resolver) and the ProgramData fallback — all incorporated. data.summary: WP-1 A-3 scoping: blocking file_lock ONLY for append_reaction + delete_message's append_deletion; group-delete + teammate-removal deletion events stay best-effort (destruction precedes event; dashboard self-heals)
data.detail: Svetlana's refinement of audit finding A-3, approved by Silvie. Blocking-and-propagating appends only where the event is the sole consistency path: message tombstones (transcript never rewritten — a lost deletion event = permanent dashboard inconsistency) and reactions (a dropped reaction is feature-data loss; retry is aggregation-idempotent). Group-delete and remove_teammate keep best-effort because the destructive action happens BEFORE the event append (recorded v0.7.0 emit-event-LAST ordering) — raising after destruction loses the event permanently since the re-entry guard rejects a retry — and the dashboard self-heals via the 5s roster/conversations reconcile. append_transcript untouched (recorded droppable-by-design). Rejected alternatives: blanket blocking for all deletion events (audit's literal direction); reordering event-before-destruction (reverses a deliberate v0.7.0 decision).

data.summary: A-1 burst fix: explicit `oldest_first` reader param, not slice-gating on `since` presence
data.detail: read_transcript/read_reactions/read_deletions got an explicit oldest_first param (via shared _window). Rejected the audit's literal "gate slice direction on whether since is set" because the group-history reader deliberately does since+newest-N, so a future since+limit history caller would be silently mis-sliced. Dashboard owns the policy: oldest_first=bool(cursor). _window also extends the cut to swallow an id-collision group at the boundary so the cursor strictly advances (no stall).

data.summary: A-3 append durability splits on "destruction-before-event", not per audit's blanket "make append_deletion blocking"
data.detail: append_reaction and delete_message's append_deletion use a blocking lock (a lost event = persistent dashboard inconsistency; retry is idempotent). Whole-group delete and teammate removal keep best-effort (block=False) because they destroy the group dir/agent record BEFORE the event (the recorded v0.7.0 emit-event-LAST ordering) — a raised append there is lost permanently on retry, strictly worse, and they self-heal on reload. 5s timeout (single-threaded server loop). append_transcript stays droppable.

The WP-1 peer review identified only group-delete; both are affected, so both got block=False. Only delete_message (tombstone, record stays resolvable) is retry-idempotent.

data.summary: Missed-event-safe wake: high-water cursor read + previous-batch id-set for boundary dedup (compute_reaction_wakes)
data.detail: To wake on an append-only event stream without missing events under burst: read since=high_water_cursor with oldest_first pagination (the window follows the cursor forward, so a burst > read-limit pages across ticks instead of scrolling past a fixed tail), and keep ONLY the previous tick's returned id-set for exact boundary dedup (the >=cursor read re-includes the boundary record each tick — comparing against last batch prevents both a perpetual re-wake and dropping a distinct same-microsecond event). Decision logic extracted to a pure function for hermetic testing. Bounded latency (limit/tick), never a drop.
context: WP-1 v0.7.1 A-2: the v0.6.1 reaction-wake re-bound known_reaction_ids to a 500-tail each 5s heartbeat → a >500 burst silently missed an author's wake. This generalizes the message-wake hardening from v0.4.x to any event sub-stream.

data.summary: WP-1 (v0.7.1): missed-event correctness — A-1 poll-cursor burst, A-2 reaction-wake cursor, A-3 durable feature appends
data.detail:
First work package of the AUDIT-v0.7.0.md backlog (Silvie plans, Svetlana implements; autonomous mode — peer-reviewed plan posted as FYI, hard gate is the diff review). Branch fix/audit-wp1, committed f64415f (1 off main).

A-1: the dashboard poll read a newest-N tail then advanced its cursor to the newest id, so any record in a >limit burst between two ~1.5s polls was sliced off AND skipped forever (transcript 200, reactions 500, deletions 1000). Fix: shared _window(records, limit, oldest_first) helper + an explicit oldest_first param on read_transcript/read_reactions/read_deletions. _api_poll passes oldest_first=bool(cursor): fresh load = newest tail (byte-identical to legacy), cursor walk = oldest window with the cursor advancing only to the last returned id (burst pages across polls). _window extends the cut to swallow an id-collision group at the boundary so the cursor strictly advances. Rejected the audit's "gate on since presence" — would mis-slice the group-history reader's deliberate since+newest-N.

A-2: the 5s heartbeat re-bound known_reaction_ids to read_reactions(limit=500) each tick, so >500 reaction events in one window scrolled the middle (incl. an author's wake target) past the tail → silent missed wake. Fix: high-water reaction_cursor drives the read (since=cursor, oldest_first); pure compute_reaction_wakes() holds only the previous tick's id-set for boundary dedup; cursor+known reset on identity change. Generalizes the v0.4.x message-wake hardening.

A-3: reactions + single-message deletions inherited the transcript's droppable file_lock_optional — but they are features, not observability, so a drop is data loss. append_reaction → blocking lock, actionable CommsError (retry folds idempotently). append_deletion → block=True default for delete_message (lost event = persistent dashboard inconsistency, retry idempotent); block=False for whole-group delete + teammate removal, which destroy state before the event by the recorded emit-event-LAST ordering and self-heal on reload. 5s timeout for the single-threaded server loop. append_transcript untouched.

Process notes: peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent (APPROVE-WITH-CHANGES; folded all must-fixes — explicit param, forward-progress collision extension, per-site A-3 scoping). Found beyond the review that remove_teammate shares group-delete's retry-loses-event hole. Baseline re-measured green at the branch point first, then full harness green with changes (env cleared of TEAMMATE_REINCARNATE_ENABLED + TEAMMATE_LAUNCH_ARGS). New hermetic test block (+160 lines) covers all three failure modes + boundaries. DESIGN.md updated (pagination, durable reaction append + bounded latency, deletion durability split, C-2/WP-7 cross-ref). Pure stdlib. Awaiting Silvie sign-off + Colton's go before merge to main.
context: WP-1 of 8. Released-state target v0.7.1 (after WP-1+WP-2).

data.summary: Missed teammate wakes (Colton-reported) = Claude Code drops channel notifications (GH #38736 mid-turn, #61797 sporadic at idle) × our single-shot emit (known_ids marks nudged-forever) — no re-nudge exists, so one dropped push = silent miss until an unrelated new message
data.detail: channel.py's reliability contract ("a dropped push loses nothing — it's recovered on the next inbox read") assumed drops are rare and agents poll; both false — an idle agent never reads unprompted, and Claude Code's channels docs promise queue-while-busy but GH #38736 documents mid-turn drops and #61797 sporadic idle drops (both unresolved). Mitigating factor: any LATER message re-emits with the full unseen count/senders/groups (v0.4.4), so misses only persist when no follow-up traffic arrives. Fix queued as WP-9 (priority after WP-2): re-nudge with backoff gated on UNSEEN ids (preserves v0.4.2 no-noise gating), emit logging for diagnosability, DESIGN contract honesty.

data.summary: CR-1: A-2 cursor conversion reintroduced a silent missed wake — pre-lock-stamped ids let contending writers commit out of id-order past the cursor
data.detail: WP-1's A-2 high-water reaction_cursor read (id >= cursor) assumed file order == id order, but reaction/deletion event ids were stamped at the CALL sites (react/delete_message/etc.) BEFORE the A-3 blocking append lock. Two contenders could commit out of order: A stamps earlier id + blocks; B stamps later id, wins lock, writes; cursor advances to B; A writes its lower id → every since=cursor read excludes it forever (no wake). The blocking locks + the dashboard ThreadingHTTPServer reacting concurrently with the single-threaded MCP loop made the window real (up to a 5s heartbeat). Caught by Silvie's adversarial review (reproduced both directions). Fix: stamp the id INSIDE append_reaction/append_deletion under the lock → file order == id order, race dies at the source.
context: review of f64415f → fixed in ea64028. Lesson: a high-water cursor over an append-only log is only missed-event-safe if append order == id order; stamp ids under the same lock that serializes the append

data.summary: WP-9 re-nudge: gate on the SAME shared unseen set as the first nudge + capped exponential backoff — provably preserves the v0.4.2 no-noise contract
data.detail: To compensate for Claude Code dropping channel pushes (GH #38736/#61797), the watcher re-emits the wake for still-unseen unread after 120/240/480s, capped at 3 attempts. Key design: run_watcher computes unseen=(unread−muted)−last_seen ONCE and both the first-nudge and re-nudge gate on that single set, so a read-but-unacked or muted message can never re-nudge — the no-noise contract is preserved by construction, not re-argued. A re-nudge is NOT a received message: personality=None, msgs_since_reminder untouched, no agent-record read (pure _wake_payload helper shared by both paths). compute_reemit is pure for hermetic testing. Rejected: re-nudging on (unseen−known_ids) [would never fire — known_ids already holds them]; an unbounded repeat [noise]; per-heartbeat instead of per-poll [120s threshold makes 0.5s vs 5s granularity irrelevant, per-poll fires within 0.5s of crossing].
context: finding H-1. Builds on the v0.4.2 read-position gating + the WP-1 compute_reaction_wakes pure-helper pattern. Plan peer-reviewed (general-purpose) + Silvie-approved.
data.author: Svetlana

data.summary: Re-nudge-on-dropped-push: an idle-wake channel can't trust a single emit — re-emit on a backoff while the trigger condition persists, gated on the same set
data.detail: When a wake is a fire-and-forget push that the client may silently drop (and the recipient never polls), one emit is not delivery. Pattern: re-emit on an exponential backoff (capped) for as long as the WAKE-WORTHY condition still holds, reusing the EXACT set/predicate the first emit used so re-nudges can't introduce noise the first nudge wouldn't. Reset the backoff when the condition clears or a new trigger arrives. Log every emit in a stable greppable line so server-emitted can be distinguished from client-dropped (evidence for an upstream bug). The honest contract: durable state is the source of truth, but recovery-on-next-read only holds if something re-wakes the idle consumer — so the producer must re-wake it.
context: teammate-comms WP-9 (6056ef9): the channel watcher's known_ids-marks-nudged-forever after one emit meant a dropped Claude Code notification = a silent permanent missed wake. Generalizes beyond this tool to any idle-push wake mechanism over a lossy channel.
data.author: Svetlana

data.summary: Shared-checkout journal drift: every implementer cognition op appends to the live journal, so a primary-tree git checkout re-blocks within seconds of any flush/alignment — worktrees are journal-immune
data.detail: With Silvie owning journal commits (never committed on a WP branch) and the implementer recording cognition as they work, the live .cognition/journal.jsonl perpetually drifts AHEAD of the last flush. So `git checkout main`/branch-switch in the shared primary tree keeps aborting ("local changes would be overwritten") even right after a verified alignment — the next curate re-drifts it. The `-text` attribute fixes the EOL-filter false-diff class but not the genuine append drift. Worktrees carry main's STATIC committed journal and never touch the live file, so they're immune — the implementer's clean default workspace for coding a WP regardless of flush timing. Lesson for the protocol: don't fight the primary tree; branch+code in a worktree, let the manager flush the live journal on their own cadence.
context: 2026-06-10. Surfaced across WP-1/WP-2/WP-9 merges+rebases all done via worktrees. Complements Silvie's journal-flush protocol + the -text/.gitattributes fix (WP-2) and the gitattributes-read-from-working-tree alignment lesson she logged.

data.summary: WP-9 (wake reliability / H-1): re-nudge unseen unread with capped backoff to survive dropped Claude Code channel pushes
data.detail:
Priority WP after WP-2 (Colton-reported: wakes sometimes missed). Root cause is a composition: Claude Code drops channel notifications (GH #38736 mid-turn, #61797 idle) × our watcher marking ids nudged-forever after one emit (known_ids |= unread_ids), while the stated "dropped push recovered on next inbox read" contract assumes an idle agent reads unprompted (it never does). So one dropped push = a silent permanent missed wake until unrelated later traffic re-wakes (why it's 'sometimes').

Fix (branch fix/audit-wp9, commit 6056ef9 off main 0bdbd09): (a) pure compute_reemit(unseen_ids, now_mono, last_emit_mono, attempts) → re-emit the wake for still-unseen unread at 120/240/480s, capped at 3, reset on caught-up/read/ack/new-emit; run_watcher computes unseen=(unread−muted)−last_seen once and shares it between first-nudge and re-nudge so the v0.4.2 no-noise contract holds by construction (a read-but-unacked or muted message can never re-nudge); a re-nudge is not a received message (personality=None, msgs_since_reminder untouched, no agent-record read; pure _wake_payload shared helper); new state reset at init + identity-change. (b) every emit logs a stable greppable stderr line `[teammate-comms] wake-emit kind=<fresh|renudge|reaction> unseen=<n> attempt=<k>` (reaction path routed through it too) for an upstream CC bug report. (c) channel.py docstring + DESIGN §7 rewrite the contract honestly, citing the two CC issues.

Plan peer-reviewed (APPROVE-WITH-CHANGES; folded: pure _wake_payload scope excluding personality bookkeeping, state reset in both sites, re-nudge placement, first-emit guard test, stderr-only logging). Hermetic compute_reemit test block: threshold fire/suppress, ×2 backoff, cap, empty-unseen reset, last_seen suppression, first-emit guard, cap-reset round-trip. Harness green ×3. Coded in a worktree (journal-immune). Awaiting Silvie review; v0.7.1 release checkpoint (WP-1+WP-2) is concurrently with Colton.
context: WP-9 of the BACKLOG (finding H-1). Released-state target batched after the v0.7.1 checkpoint.

data.summary: WP-9 retro-nudge-after-unmute (v0.4.2 sin): the re-nudge clock was armed by the caught-up NON-emit, so an arrived-while-muted message re-nudged after unmute
data.detail: compute_reemit's empty-unseen branch originally returned (False, 0, now_mono) — re-stamping last_emit_mono every caught-up tick. A message arriving in a muted group keeps unseen empty (correct, no fresh nudge; known_ids absorbs it) but that branch kept ARMING the clock from a non-emit. On unmute the absorbed message reappeared in unseen with fresh empty (already in known_ids) and fell into the re-nudge path with an armed clock → re-nudged a message never first-nudged (main emits [], the branch emitted 3). Caught by Silvie's adversarial reviewer simulating both directions. Root cause: the gate had no "was this batch ever actually emitted?" notion. Fix (e1e6a45): empty-unseen DISARMS (return None), so last_emit_mono is non-None only after a real fresh emit; the first-emit guard then keeps never-emitted batches (unmute-reveal, seed window) silent. Lesson: an idle-wake backoff clock must be armed ONLY by a real emit, never by a caught-up/reset path — otherwise reset-then-reveal retro-fires.
context: teammate-comms WP-9 review of 6056ef9 → fixed in e1e6a45. The v0.4.2 no-noise contract ("an unmute never retro-nudges") that the message-wake path was hardened for — the re-nudge feature briefly reintroduced it. Sibling discipline to the WP-1 CR-1 (stamp ids under the lock): both are "what arms/orders the state must be the real event

data.summary: In-process dashboard tests must bind port=0, not the default 7842: allow_reuse_address lets the test collide with a LIVE dashboard and race requests to the wrong (different-token) server
data.detail: WP-3's dashboard HTTP test first failed uniformly 403/401 with a token that provably matched the server's. Root cause: _DashboardServer sets allow_reuse_address=True, and start_dashboard defaults to port 7842; on Windows SO_REUSEADDR allows binding a port another process already holds, so the test bound 7842 alongside Colton's real running dashboard and client connections raced to his server (whose token differs). Fix: start_dashboard(..., port=0) → OS-assigned ephemeral port, hermetic regardless of what's on 7842. General lesson: any test that starts a real reuse-addr server on a well-known port can silently talk to a co-resident instance — use port=0 for in-process server tests.
context: dashboard.py start_dashboard / _DashboardServer.allow_reuse_address. Surfaced because Colton dogfoods the plugin (a live dashboard on 7842 during the test run).

data.summary: The harness report print is not unicode-safe: print('  -', f) hits cp1252 stdout on Windows and crashes on any non-ASCII char in a failure message (latent G-3)
data.detail: A WP-3 failure string containing '→' (U+2192) crashed the whole harness run with UnicodeEncodeError in the failure-report loop — Windows Python defaults stdout to cp1252. The real test failure was masked by the print crash. Worked around by using ASCII ('->') in WP-3 failure strings, but the report function itself remains fragile: any future non-ASCII failure message crashes the run instead of reporting. Proper fix (G-3 harness-robustness sweep, WP-8): reconfigure stdout to utf-8 (sys.stdout.reconfigure(encoding='utf-8', errors='replace')) at harness start, or encode failure lines defensively. Convention meanwhile: keep failure strings ASCII.
context: tests/test_handshake.py report loop (~line 1386). Relates to audit G-3 (harness brittleness). Discovered while debugging the dashboard port-collision (the crash hid the real 403/401 failure).

data.summary: os.replace of a DIRECTORY is not exclusive under concurrency on Windows (4/8 concurrent renamers 'won'); use mkdir for an atomic exclusive claim instead
data.detail: WP-6's A-7 lock-steal needed exactly-one-winner among N concurrent stealers. The plan/review specified the claim as os.replace-renaming the stale lock dir to a unique name (only one rename of the same source should succeed). On Windows that did NOT hold — a deterministic test of 8 threads each os.replace(lock_dir, unique_scratch) saw FOUR return success (must be one). os.replace/MoveFileEx of a directory is not reliably exclusive under concurrent renames of the same source on Windows. The mkdir primitive IS atomic-exclusive cross-platform (it's why the lock itself is mkdir-based), so the fix claims via `mkdir(<lock>.claim)` — exactly one of N concurrent mkdirs wins (FileExistsError for the rest) — with a post-claim lock_dir.exists() recheck for the sequential-call case. Lesson: for an exactly-one-winner atomic claim, prefer mkdir (proven exclusive) over directory rename (not exclusive on Windows).
context: file_lock steal-from-dead-holder. Surfaced by the 8-stealer exactly-one-winner test failing 4/8 on Windows with the os.replace approach. Relates to the file_lock mkdir-based design.
data.author: Svetlana

data.summary: WP-6 (race + scale pt1): A-7 dead-only atomic lock steal, A-5 predicate group-purge, A-4 doc; C-1+N-1 deferred to a WP-7 seek-from-tail cluster
data.detail:
Audit A-7/A-5/A-4 + C-1/N-1 (commit 26421d7 off main 23f8a2c, fix/audit-wp6). The concurrency-heaviest WP; adversarially peer-reviewed (deep interleaving sim) which reshaped it.

A-7: file_lock now records the holder PID in the lock dir and on timeout steals ONLY from a verified-dead holder (`_pid_alive(pid) is False` exactly — alive/None/no-pid never stolen; no-pid bounded by the timeout-raise). The atomic claim is a mkdir of a sibling <lock>.claim marker (exactly-one-winner) AFTER discovering os.replace-of-directory is non-exclusive on Windows (see discovery f05346eddb2b). Behavior change: an alive-but-slow holder now raises instead of being stolen from; file_lock_optional stays never-raise/droppable. The empirical reachability of the old unconditional steal was WP-1's CR-1 flake.

A-5: whole-group delete purges member inboxes by the group==sigil PREDICATE (new remove_group_messages_from_inbox; the old id-snapshot remove_messages_from_inbox removed as dead). This shrinks the race window (catches a copy landing before the purge) but does NOT close it — a send_group fanning out after the purge can still orphan a copy; that residual is documented as eventually-consistent (cross-store atomicity the v0.7.0 plan rejected). Framed 'window shrunk, residual documented', not 'race fixed'.

A-4: documented eventual-consistency (mid-fan-out joiner keeps a copy).

C-1 and N-1 DEFERRED to WP-7 (rule-#5 pushback): C-1's 'bounded tail scan' is a no-op because read_transcript parses the whole file to build its window (confirmed) — the real fix is a seek-from-tail read, exactly C-2's WP-7 mechanism. N-1's transcript id IS the message id (can't re-stamp under the transcript lock), so the real fix is a file-offset/byte cursor that must co-design with WP-7's rotation. Both documented + an N-1 characterization test. So WP-7 becomes a 'seek-from-tail + byte-cursor + rotation' cluster (C-1 + N-1(c) + C-2 + C-3).

Tests: A-7 alive-not-stolen/dead-stolen/exactly-one-winner-under-8-stealers/None-not-stolen; A-5 predicate removes group copies (incl. a raced-in one) keeps DMs; N-1 characterization. Harness green ×5 (contention gate). Seventh WP this session.

data.summary: A-7 hardening (WP-6 CR): age-gate the mkdir steal-claim (orphaned claim can't permanently stick a dead lock) + host-gate liveness (never steal a remote holder, local _pid_alive can't judge it)
data.detail: Silvie's adversarial review of the A-7 mkdir-claim found two failure modes, both 'what happens when a steal can't complete or can't be trusted'. CR-1: a stealer killed between claim.mkdir and its finally leaks an orphaned <lock>.claim → every future _claim_if_dead returns False → a verified-dead holder's lock is permanently stuck (worse than the old blind steal, which recovered). Fix: age-gate — on the claim FileExistsError path, if the existing claim's mtime > CLAIM_STALE_SECONDS (30s; claims live microseconds), rmdir + retry the mkdir once (two cleaners race the fresh mkdir, exactly one wins). CR-2: _pid_alive is purely local, so on a shared cross-host comms root a live remote holder reads locally-dead → stolen → two writers (A-7 relocated cross-host). Fix: the pid file records pid AND host; steal only when host == socket.gethostname() AND verified dead — mirrors is_channel_alive's existing host-gated pid trust (the lock pid-check was the one liveness check that forgot the pattern). Consequence documented: a dead REMOTE holder's lock is recovered only manually (bounded by raise/drop). Lesson: any local-pid liveness check on a shared-root resource must be host-gated; any exclusive-claim marker needs an age-gate so a mid-claim death can't wedge the resource.
context: teammate-comms WP-6 CR (commit 53f2ac0 on top of 26421d7). Hardens the A-7 file_lock dead-holder steal. Relates to the mkdir-claim decision + the os.replace-non-exclusive discovery
and to is_channel_alive's host-gated pid pattern.
data.author: Svetlana

data.summary: Robust mkdir-lock dead-holder steal: pid+host-gated, dead-only, exclusive mkdir-claim, age-gated self-heal — a reusable cross-platform file-lock recovery design
data.detail: A mkdir-based file lock that can SAFELY recover from a crashed holder without ever risking two writers. The full discipline: (1) the lock dir records the holder's pid AND host. (2) On timeout, steal ONLY from a holder on THIS host (host == gethostname() — a remote pid is unknowable locally) whose pid is _pid_alive(pid) is False EXACTLY (alive=True and undetermined=None both forbid stealing — absence of a death-proof is not a death-proof). (3) The steal's exclusive claim is a mkdir of a sibling <lock>.claim marker — the SAME atomic primitive the lock uses, so exactly one of N concurrent stealers wins (NOT os.replace-of-directory, which is non-exclusive on Windows; NOT rmdir+mkdir, which lets two 'win'). (4) After winning the claim, re-check the lock still exists (a later caller mustn't win a lock an earlier stealer removed). (5) The claim is AGE-GATED: an orphan older than ~30s (a stealer killed mid-claim) is reclaimed (rmdir + one retry, two cleaners race the fresh mkdir) so the lock can't get permanently stuck behind a dead claim. (6) The optional variant never raises (yields False); the blocking variant raises on a non-steal so the contention surfaces. Net: a slow-but-alive (local or remote) holder is never stolen from; only a verified-dead local holder is, exactly once, self-healing. Mirrors is_channel_alive's host-gated pid trust.
context: commits 26421d7 + 53f2ac0). Silvie flagged this design for reuse. Built from the os.replace-non-exclusive-on-Windows discovery + the claim-leak/cross-host CRs.

data.summary: WP-7 P1 (C-1): read_jsonl_tail — BINARY backward-chunk NDJSON tail reader, byte-identical to read_transcript[-N:] but reads only the file tail
data.detail: Seek from EOF, read backward in chunk_size blocks, carry a `leftover` partial leading line across chunks so multibyte UTF-8 chars and records straddling a chunk boundary rejoin before decode+strip. 'Newest N' = N PARSEABLE records (blank/garbage skipped), not N raw lines. Binary mode is mandatory — text-mode seek/tell lies under CRLF translation. Wired as a no-cursor/forward-only fast path into read_transcript/read_reactions/read_deletions, and tools._scan_transcript_for_id (tail-first @ _RESOLVE_TAIL=500, full-scan fallback only when the window is saturated and missed) replaces the two whole-transcript reverse scans in react()/resolve_message(). TEAMMATE_TRANSCRIPT=0 → empty tail → None, so group/inbox fallbacks run unchanged.

data.summary: WP-7 P2 (C-2): bound deletions.jsonl via a target-keyed set-file; fresh dashboard load reads baseline-set + FULL live jsonl (not the 1000 tail) so completeness is gate-independent
data.detail: Compaction folds all-but-newest-RETAIN deletion events into deletions_set.json (dict target->event, union by target), writing the set FIRST (atomic) then trimming the jsonl (atomic temp+replace) — both best-effort, never failing a delete (the event append is the durable op). Triggered inside append_deletion (both block paths) under the held lock via a cheap getsize gate. KEY FIX from peer review: the fresh-load _api_poll read is FULL-FILE (read_deletions limit=None), not the bounded newest-1000 tail — otherwise an un-folded middle (jsonl between RETAIN and the byte-gate) is skipped by both the tail AND the stale set = C-2 not closed. With the full read, baseline-set ∪ full-jsonl = every deletion ever regardless of gate timing; the gate is purely a cost knob. Rejected: bounded-tail fresh read (gap); count-based gate (full-read-per-append cost). Residual self-healing EC: a cursored client lagging >RETAIN deletions between polls skips compacted-away tombstones until a full reload — documented, optional cheap fallback offered to Silvie.
context: branch fix/audit-wp7. C-2: dashboard fresh load only read newest 1000 deletion events
so >1000 historical deletes made old tombstones reappear. Plan peer-reviewed by a general-purpose subagent (CHANGE-REQUESTED → APPROVE-WITH-NITS after the full-file-fresh-read fix).

data.summary: WP-7 P2 (C-2) shipped: deletions.jsonl compaction — target-keyed set-file baseline + full-file fresh load so an old tombstone can't reappear on the dashboard
data.detail: Audit C-2: a fresh dashboard load replayed only the newest 1000 deletion events, so a message deleted past that window reappeared. Fix: _compact_deletions_locked folds all-but-newest-DELETIONS_RETAIN(1000) events into a target-keyed deletions_set.json (union by target; deletions are monotonic), writing the SET FIRST (atomic) then trimming the jsonl (atomic temp+replace, orphan-tmp cleanup). _maybe_compact_deletions: a cheap getsize gate wired into BOTH append_deletion paths under the held lock, fully self-contained and NEVER raising (the event append is the durable op; compaction is best-effort size relief). dashboard _api_poll fresh load now unions the set-file baseline with the FULL live jsonl (read_deletions limit=None) — NOT the bounded tail. The peer review (general-purpose subagent) first returned CHANGE-REQUESTED: the originally-planned bounded-1000 fresh read left a gap (an un-folded middle event between the retained tail and the byte gate was in neither the tail nor the stale set). Switching the fresh read to full-file made completeness gate-independent (the gate is now purely a size knob) → APPROVE-WITH-NITS. Residual self-healing ECs documented in DESIGN.md: a cursored client lagging >RETAIN deletions between polls skips folded tombstones until full reload; group-recreate-same-name widened replay window; contention-skip. Tests cover trim+fold, completeness incl oldest, the gap case, idempotent re-compact, block=False never-raises, corrupt-set->{}, and a real loopback _api_poll pinning fresh/caught-up/lagged. data.summary: WP-7 P2 CR: rescue a lagged cursored dashboard IN-POLL (dcursor<floor → replay full baseline∪jsonl that poll) instead of documenting a reload-to-recover gap
data.detail: Silvie overrode my document-and-self-heal default: "recovers on next full reload" is self-CONCEALING not self-healing — a suspended browser tab is everyday and a message rendering undeleted until the user happens to F5 contradicts the delete feature. Fix: _api_poll reads the bounded live jsonl once (floor=oldest id), and on a FRESH load OR a LAGGED cursor (dcursor < floor) replays the complete baseline∪jsonl union; the rescue fires once because new_dcursor then advances past the floor → steady-state incremental polls pay nothing extra (cost = same single full read the old cursored since-scan already did). Incremental walk reuses the shared comms._window for collision-safe burst paging (last-returned-id advance). Test flipped to a HOSTILE rescue assertion (compact past a cursored client's dcursor → the tombstone ARRIVES in that poll, not after reload). Rejected: cheap-but-stale documented EC (default).

data.summary: WP-7 P3 shipped: transcript BYTE cursor (offset|crc32-of-first-line) — live dashboard polls stream only new bytes, and audit N-1 (out-of-order tee) is fixed by making id order irrelevant
data.detail: The dashboard records stream paged by id `since` re-scanned the whole growing transcript every cursored poll and silently skipped an out-of-order tee (record teed after the cursor passed its id). Replaced with comms.read_transcript_after(path, offset, generation, limit) -> (records, new_offset, new_generation, reset): a forward byte-streaming reader. generation = zlib.crc32 of the first line (bytes 0..first \n) — append-stable (byte 0 never moves on the append-only log), recreation-sensitive; rejected st_ctime (changes on append on Linux → reset-thrash) and inode (reuse gap). Resets (transparent re-tail) on the ""/torn-head sentinel, gen mismatch, or out-of-range offset (incl. negative — caught in the diff-gate, was a livelock). ONE stat/poll; gen-read and increment-read use separate opens with absolute seeks. new_offset advances by RAW newline geometry (byte-exact under CRLF/blank/garbage/multibyte, always lands on \n boundary; partial final line deferred = torn-tail safe; burst>limit pages out). transcript_tail_and_cursor mints fresh via stat-THEN-tail (offset<=tail size → race-window append shown AND re-streamed+deduped, never lost). dashboard _api_poll records branch rewired; frontend cursor documented opaque (seen-set dedups the re-tail → no protocol change). N-1 now fixed: a late tee lands at EOF so byte-streaming emits it in arrival order ("shown late beats never shown"). NDJSON rotation stays deferred to WP-10 with a generation contract written to receive it. Plan adversarially reviewed (4 blockers fixed: torn-head, "" sentinel fall-through, lossy mint ordering, parsed-text offset reconstruction); diff adversarially reviewed (negative-offset livelock + 3 test gaps closed). Tests: read_transcript_after matrix with byte-exact new_offset assertions as the centerpiece + real loopback _api_poll. Harness green. Commit a9f3af7. WP-7 closes on Silvie's sign-off.

data.summary: A-7 steal-defense reintroduced its own two-writers race: _claim_if_dead acted on STALE death-evidence (pid read at top, guarded post-claim only by lock_dir.exists())
data.detail: Between the top-of-function pid read and winning the exclusive .claim, an earlier stealer can remove the dead lock and re-acquire a fresh LIVE lock; exists() can't distinguish it from the old dead lock, so the later stealer rmtree's a live holder's lock → two writers (the exact A-7 corruption the defense exists to prevent). Surfaced as a flaky "2 stealers won the atomic claim" caught at the v0.8.0 release gate by Silvie's independent run. Fix (bf064e9): RE-READ + RE-VERIFY the pid (same-host + verified-dead) UNDER the claim — the held claim + still-present dead dir (which blocks any re-acquire mkdir) make the pid file stable, so the re-read is trustworthy; anything else → drop, don't steal. data.summary: WP-12: removed _renudge_ids gate → content-agnostic re-nudge; GROUP wakes now recover from drops; no-noise contract preserved via unseen_ids + unchanged first-emit guard
data.detail: The old _renudge_ids set prevented re-nudging if any message had been nudged before, which silently blocked GROUP wake recovery after a dropped push. Removing the gate makes re-nudge content-agnostic. Noise is still gated at a lower level: unseen_ids = (unread − muted) − last_seen, so already-seen messages never re-emit, and the first-emit guard prevents duplicate wakes when nothing changed.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: WP-12: Identity._generation bumped on every .set(); watcher resets known_ids and clocks on generation change — repairs same-name re-register after compaction losing wakes
data.detail: After Claude Code compaction, a re-register with the same agent name didn't change last_agent so the watcher's state was never reset; the stale known_ids cursor meant all pre-compaction messages were silently skipped. Adding a generation counter (bumped every .set()) and resetting watcher state on generation change makes compaction-triggered re-registration reliably restart the watcher's view of the inbox.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: Daemon loop try/except must back off inside the except (stop_evt.wait) — defensive hardening that swaps thread-death for a CPU-spin at 100% is worse than the original failure
data.detail: Adding a bare try/except around a daemon poll loop prevents crash but introduces a busy-spin if the error is persistent. The correct fix is stop_evt.wait(POLL_SECONDS) inside the except block — the wait backs off at normal poll cadence and returns immediately on stop. Silvie's manager review caught the missing wait in the WP-12 PR before merge and held the fix for the one-liner addition.
data.author: Colton Dyck

data.summary: WP-12 / v0.8.2 comms-stability: watcher TypeError crash + group recovery + compaction reset + busy-spin hardening
data.detail: Full arc: WP-11a's leanness pass removed the personality positional arg from emit_channel_event but left a stray None at the re-nudge call site in run_watcher (channel.py). The daemon thread had no try/except at the time, so the first re-nudge of any unacked DM/urgent/@mention crashed the entire watcher — heartbeat stopped, agent appeared offline, all subsequent channel wakes died until session restart. Diagnosed via 3 Explore investigations + adversarial review. Fix delivered in 4 parts: (1) drop the stray None from the re-nudge emit; (2) remove the _renudge_ids gate so GROUP wakes can recover from dropped pushes (no-noise still held via unseen_ids + first-emit guard); (3) add Identity._generation counter (bumped every .set()) so the watcher resets known_ids/clocks on same-name re-register after compaction; (4) wrap the watcher while-loop body in try/except with stop_evt.wait(POLL_SECONDS) in the except to prevent CPU-spin on persistent errors. Silvie's manager review held the PR for the busy-spin one-liner (item 4) before approving at SHA 8b66b93. PR #7 merged with --match-head-commit enforcement; main HEAD 79dafb4. Live-watcher integration tests added to test_handshake.py — the fatal re-nudge path had zero test coverage before this work. Loki re-pinned marketplace to 66d517a. Live-validated on the released build by Silvie + Svetlana: smoke round-trip, re-nudge survival + GROUP recovery, watcher survival, and message-during-AskUserQuestion-block recovery confirmed twice (the original reported symptom). No tool-surface change. Lesson: a leanness param removal left a call site mismatched and no test drove the live re-nudge path, so a fatal crash passed CI green — integration/daemon paths need at least one real-drive test, not just pure-unit coverage.

data.summary: A-7 file_lock dead-holder steal: four-iteration hardening arc → pid+host-gated, age-gated, TOCTOU-safe mkdir-claim design
data.detail: Four iterations across WP-6 and post-v0.8.0 hardened the dead-holder steal from a naive unconditional steal to a production-safe design. Iteration 1 (WP-6): the steal path was empirically reachable under burst contention; os.replace of a lock directory was replaced with an exclusive mkdir claim, pid-gated to dead-only holders. Iteration 2 (WP-6 CR, Silvie adversarial review): two new failure modes surfaced — a stealer killed mid-claim leaves an orphaned .claim blocking future steals (fix: 30s age-gate); a remote holder's pid reads locally-dead on a shared root (fix: host-gate, never steal a non-local holder). Iteration 3 (v0.8.0 release gate): independent run exposed a TOCTOU — _claim_if_dead read the pid at function entry but only re-checked lock_dir.exists() post-claim, so a live re-acquirer could be stolen from; fix was re-verify the pid under the claim. Iteration 4 (post-v0.8.0): the A-7 contention test was rmtree-fragile on Windows; re-aimed at the production property (no two holders + ≥1 acquirer) rather than the claim-count artifact. The resulting pattern (51f70ca10a2a) is a reusable cross-platform file-lock recovery design.
data.author: Colton Dyck

Membership roster is computed live by grouping agent records on the normalized project key (humans excluded) rather than stored on the profile — storing a member list would recreate the exact fragmentation the feature removes. data.summary: WP-13 load-bearing fix: validate_project_key must normalize \→/ + case-fold before compare/slug — else cross-OS agents silently split the roster
data.detail: Project key = the existing project label, which is auto-filled from $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR — backslash-separated on Windows, slash on Unix. Without normalization (\→/, case-fold, reject fs-unsafe chars), two agents in the same dir on different OSes never match each other's key and vanish from the roster; the same normalization makes key→filename slug injective (no collisions). Roster derivation MUST run each agent's project through the same function before comparing. Peer review (sonnet) flagged this as the top correctness risk; acceptance test §9.2 asserts convergence under the actual mismatch. Also locked: team-scoped projects/ dir, BLOCKING lock on writes (append_reaction pattern, not file_lock_optional), advisory edit convention (no hard gate).

data.summary: WP-13 gate gotcha: test_wp13_projects.py server-roundtrip needs `uv run` (installed pkg); bare python → ModuleNotFoundError → Errno22 stdin flush, masquerading as a server crash

Run via `uv run --no-dev python tests/test_wp13_projects.py`. Bare `python` makes the subprocess die with ModuleNotFoundError, which surfaces only as OSError [Errno 22] on the harness's proc.stdin.flush() — looks like a deterministic server crash but is an environment artifact. data.summary: Timing-tautology gate trap: a test asserting two-now_timestamp() calls differ is flaky-tautological — back-to-back datetime.now() returns the SAME microsecond on a fast/idle path, so it passes against the buggy code it's meant to catch
data.detail: WP-13 verb fix proof asserted created_at==updated_at on create. Reverting to the two-call bug, the test still passed 6/6 because the two datetime.now() calls coincided with no latency between them; the bug only manifests under inter-call delay (an adversarial run under concurrent I/O saw them differ). A tautology guard whose failure depends on timing is not a valid AC-8 proof. Fix: inject the clock — monkeypatch now_timestamp to a distinct-value counter so reverted (2-call) code deterministically yields t0!=t1 and fixed (1-call) code yields t0==t0. Always run the actual revert at the gate; never reason about whether a timing-sensitive test would fail.
context: Gating WP-13 project profiles at SHA 2bdf3a3. Caught the verb tautology by running the revert and characterizing the timestamp flake 6x on Windows.

data.summary: WP-13 project profiles (v0.9.0) — shipped: 4 tools, derived-live roster, cross-OS key fix; 3 gate cycles caught an env-artifact false-fail and a tautological verb proof
data.detail:
Goal: add a first-class project-profile layer over the per-agent `project` field so agents discover which projects exist + who to ask, without fragmentation. Design (Silvie, peer-reviewed by sonnet): membership DERIVED live (never stored), keyed by the existing normalized project label; 4 tools (project_register upsert under blocking lock, list_projects = name+roster+summary+aggregates, project_profile full detail, project_delete); advisory-not-enforced edit convention. Load-bearing correctness: validate_project_key normalizes \\→/ + case-folds + forbids fs-unsafe & % chars → injective key→slug via urllib.parse.quote(safe=''); roster derivation and dashboard grouping normalize through the same function (fixes cross-OS roster split). Implementer: Svetlana.

Gate (3 cycles, manager creed = own test at pinned SHA is the fact):
- Cycle 1 @ 6e4f322: my first run FAILED 3/3 — traced to an ENV ARTIFACT (server-roundtrip test needs `uv run --no-dev`; bare python can't import the spawned subprocess → dies as OSError Errno22 on stdin.flush, masquerading as a crash). Re-run under uv: green. My read + adversarial sonnet pass found: verb bug (project_register always says 'Updated' on create — two now_timestamp() calls), list_projects silently drops un-normalizable agents (violates §6.2), over-broad except CommsError. Required all 3.
- Cycle 2 @ 2bdf3a3: fixes correct, but the verb PROOF was tautological — reverting to the bug, created_at==updated_at still held 6/6 (back-to-back datetime.now() returns same microsecond with no latency; bug is latency-dependent). Required a deterministic clock-injected proof.
- Cycle 3 @ b91127e: clock injection (stepped t0,t1,... via monkeypatched now_timestamp, restored in finally) reliably fails 3/3 on revert. SIGNED OFF.

Ship: PR #8 merged with --match-head-commit b91127e → main @ 69080b9; CI green ubuntu+windows; version 0.9.0 synced; docs complete. Marketplace re-pin handed to Loki (his authority). Two durable lessons noded: the uv-run gate gotcha and the timing-tautology trap.

data.summary: WP-14 avatars: keep MCP zero-dep by making Pillow an optional extra lazy-imported only in set-avatar; ingest pre-renders PNG+ANSI+ASCII sidecars so all serve/read paths stay stdlib
data.detail: The avatar feature needs an image lib (decode/resize/pad/encode) but the MCP server is deliberately dependencies=[] (instant spawn, no stdio-handshake-blocking dep tree). Resolution chosen: (a) Pillow is an optional [images] extra, imported lazily ONLY inside the set-avatar handler (avatars.py ingest_avatar) — missing Pillow raises a clear install-guidance CommsError, never an import error on spawn. (b) Ingest PRE-RENDERS and caches every consumer artifact as sidecars in a team-scoped avatars/ dir (<name>.png 256x256 black-padded, <name>.ansi xterm-256-color half-block strip, <name>.txt mono ASCII) so dashboard + statusline only ever read already-rendered bytes — zero image lib at read time. (c) Agent JSON holds only avatar={hash,updated_at}, never the bytes. Rejected alternatives: storing base64 bytes inline in agent record (bloats every read_agent_record/teammate_list/api read); making Pillow a hard dep (breaks the instant-spawn invariant); rendering ANSI/ASCII on demand at read (drags an image lib onto the hot path). Two-level lazy-import discipline is load-bearing: avatars.py imports PIL only inside ingest_avatar AND tools.py imports avatars lazily inside _handle_set_avatar (never at module top) — either one leaking would load PIL on spawn. Statusline uses xterm 256-color (not 24-bit truecolor — Claude Code statusLine documents only basic ANSI) via a new `avatar` subcommand at top of server.main() that reads cached sidecars with no Pillow/no network.
context: teammate-comms WP-14 kickoff. Colton gave go 2026-06-29 (WP-13 shipped first as v0.9.0). Manager Silvie planned + peer-reviewed (sonnet
8 findings fixed in v2); Svetlana implementing. Brief doc:69ab9acc9f0a.

data.summary: WP-14 — Teammate profile images (avatars) — peer-reviewed brief v2
data.detail:
# WP-14 — Teammate profile images (avatars)

Manager: Silvie · Implementer: Svetlana · Status: Peer-reviewed + revised (v2), Colton gave go 2026-06-29.
Branch: feat/wp14-teammate-avatars (off origin/main @ 4a1e4a7, v0.9.0). Version target: 0.10.0.

## 1. Goal
Let a teammate OPTIONALLY attach a small profile image. Renders in the dashboard roster next to the agent; discoverable by a Claude Code statusline as a tiny terminal-rendered thumbnail (ANSI color half-blocks, monochrome ASCII fallback). Ingestion forgiving: any image forced to 256x256, aspect preserved, black-padded if non-square. Default-off; absence changes nothing.

## 2. Load-bearing constraint
MCP server is deliberately zero-dependency pure stdlib (dependencies=[] — instant spawn, no dep tree blocking stdio handshake). No image lib on the hot path. Ingestion needs Pillow -> optional extra, lazy-imported in the set-avatar handler only. Everything downstream of ingest is dependency-free because ingest pre-renders+caches every consumer artifact (PNG, ANSI strip, ASCII strip). Core: separate heavy ingest path from light serve path, cache at ingest.

## 3. Locked decisions
1. Image bytes NOT in agent JSON — bytes in team-scoped avatars/ dir; record holds only hash+timestamp. 2. Pillow optional, lazy in set-avatar; missing -> clear CommsError. 3. Pre-render 3 sidecars at ingest (PNG, ANSI, ASCII). 4. Input is a filesystem path (optional image_base64 fallback). 5. Default-off.

## 4. Data model
New team-scoped avatars/ dir (mirror get_agents_dir). <name>.png 256x256 RGB black-padded; <name>.ansi 256-color half-block; <name>.txt mono ASCII. get_avatars_dir helper. Agent record gains avatar={"hash":sha256-12,"updated_at":iso} via write_agent_record merge (NOT a PROFILE_FIELDS entry). Hash = cache-buster + presence flag.

## 5. Ingestion (only Pillow-touching code) — avatars.py ingest_avatar(root,team,name,*,path,image_base64)
(1) Lazy import PIL; ImportError->CommsError. (2) 50MB byte cap before decode; set Image

data.summary: Cognition writes can be silently lost after a /mcp reconnect: record/add_task/store_document returned success + IDs, but a later full reload reverted the graph (162->159), dropping all 3; embeddings orphaned (162 vs graph 159)
data.detail: During WP-14 kickoff, immediately after a `/mcp` reconnect of plugin:vibe-cognition, I recorded a decision + task + document. All three returned success with node IDs, and cognition_get_uncurated_nodes + a first get_status (162 nodes, document:1, task:1) showed them present. Minutes later get_status reported 159 nodes (document:0, task:0) and cognition_reload confirmed the on-disk journal authoritatively held only 159 — the three writes had NOT durably landed in journal.jsonl, even though the embedding store still counted 162 (orphaned embeddings = the tell). Edges referencing the lost IDs failed with 'Missing from_id/to_id'. Likely cause: a flush/persistence gap right after the MCP reconnect (in-memory + embedding write succeeded, journal append did not), possibly aggravated by a concurrent same-repo session (Svetlana live in teammate-comms) since the store auto-re-replays the shared journal before every op. FIX THAT WORKED: re-record each node, then call cognition_reload immediately and confirm nodes_after stayed bumped (159->160->...->162). LESSON: after recording right after a reconnect (or in a busy shared-journal repo), verify durability with cognition_reload before relying on the node — a success return is not proof of journal persistence. New durable IDs: decision de7f0a21781e, document 34acde2df471 (doc:69ab9acc9f0a), task 42885e93a464; lost IDs were 53cc98c84730 / 453a57e41767 / 6729870bf145.

data.summary: Sibling serve-path guard asymmetry: WP-14 dashboard /avatar validated the agent name vs traversal, but the statusline CLI building the same sidecar path did not → arbitrary *.ansi/*.txt read. When one input path is guarded, audit ALL sibling paths.
data.detail:
teammate-comms exposes the avatar sidecar via TWO paths: (1) dashboard HTTP _api_avatar, (2) statusline CLI _avatar_subcommand. Both build `get_avatars_dir(root,team)/f"{name}.{ext}"`. The HTTP path called validate_agent_name(name) before the join (rejecting .., /, \, nulls); the CLI path did not, so `--name ../../x` resolved out-of-tree and read_text leaked file contents to stdout (exit 0). Caught in the gate by an adversarial reviewer + confirmed with a live sentinel PoC, fixed in 717c9b1.

REUSABLE LESSON: a validation guard on one consumer of a shared path-building helper does NOT protect other consumers. When you see input sanitized before a filesystem/URL/SQL construction, grep for every other call site of the same construction and confirm each guards independently. Centralizing the guard inside the path helper (get_avatars_dir refusing unsafe segments) would make the safe path the default and kill the whole class — candidate follow-up.

Also: the gate's value here was concrete — own-read + adversarial subagent + a real live PoC found a security defect a handed-green ('44 checks green') would have shipped. Mutation-testing the fix's new test confirmed it actually catches the regression.
context: Found during WP-14 manager gate. Dashboard path dashboard.py:226 guarded; CLI server.py _avatar_subcommand unguarded until 717c9b1.

data.detail: Idea from Colton (2026-06-30): messages that are read but not acked can linger in the inbox and get re-retrieved on subsequent 'check for new messages' calls, re-injecting the same content and burning context tokens over a long session.
